ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product entry; no specific basil-marinated deep-fried variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked chicken wings broadly; no basil-infused or deep-fried variant code exists. Closest defensible match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for basil chicken wings as a prepared dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 5 pieces of chicken wings weighing approximately 250 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu for this item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken wing, cooked, fried (SR Legacy ID 05079) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food Database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken wing (ID 05079). Sodium, sugar, and micronutrient values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition database for comparable fried chicken preparations. Values adjusted upward for basil-sauce marinade contribution (fish sauce, soy sauce) consistent with HPB fried chicken wing with sauce entries. Trans fat estimated at 0.2 g/100g consistent with commercial deep-frying oil usage documented in HPB database. All values are plausible for a deep-fried, sauce-tossed chicken wing product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for fried chicken with sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for basil chicken wings. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous fried chicken with sauce entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) published in Diabetes Care. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content (18 g each per 100 g) with only 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, consistent with high-protein, high-fat fried poultry preparations. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.0) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3. Serving carbohydrate = 6.0 g/100g × 250 g / 100 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 480 mg/100g (1200 mg per 5-piece serving) exceeds recommended single-meal sodium limits for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of basil chicken wings to no more than 2–3 pieces per occasion, avoid additional dipping sauces, and request reduced-sodium soy sauce and less fish sauce to reduce sodium load below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6 g/100g, GI ~35) is favourable for glycaemic control, but high fat content (18 g/100g) from deep-frying may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Basil chicken wings have a low glycaemic index and low carbohydrate load suitable for occasional consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes, but the high fat content (18 g/100g) and deep-fried preparation should limit frequency to no more than once or twice per week to avoid exacerbating insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.8 g/100g, trans fat at 0.2 g/100g, cholesterol at 80 mg/100g, and sodium at 480 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat basil chicken wings as an occasional food only, limiting to 2–3 pieces per occasion, given the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium that can elevate LDL cholesterol, promote arterial stiffness, and increase blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat at 0.2 g/100g amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried basil chicken wings due to AGE generation and trans fat content; a baked or grilled preparation retaining the basil marinade is a preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil Chicken Wings (九层塔鸡翅膀) is a popular fried poultry dish sold in Singapore hawker centres and food delivery platforms, featuring five pieces of chicken wings marinated and deep-fried or pan-fried, then tossed in a fragrant sauce made from Thai holy basil (kra pao), fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, chilli, and oyster sauce. The dish draws on Thai culinary tradition — particularly the flavour profile of pad kra pao — adapted for the Singaporean palate. The wings are characterised by a crispy, golden exterior from deep-frying, a juicy and tender interior, and a savoury-sweet-spicy sauce with a pronounced herbal aroma from the basil. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 260 kcal, 18 g protein, 18 g fat (4.8 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), and 6 g carbohydrate with a low glycaemic index of approximately 35. Sodium is elevated at 480 mg/100g due to fish sauce and soy sauce, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high fat content from deep-frying is a consideration for individuals with type 2 diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving of 5 pieces (250 g) delivers approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 45 g fat, and 15 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of approximately 5.3.

confidence_reason: Core macros are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried chicken wing data (ID 05079) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore comparable fried chicken entries. The basil sauce contribution to sodium and sugar is estimated from HPB sauce-tossed fried chicken analogues. No exact approved-source entry for basil chicken wings exists; values are defensible adaptations. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried chicken with sauce entries. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact dish match in any approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried basil-sauce chicken wing preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition database as of 2026-04-16. The dish is a Thai-Singaporean fusion preparation sold under menu code 104D on GrabFood Singapore. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Curry', 'Pad Thai') were removed as they are not applicable to this dish. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore were used instead. Halal status is assumed based on typical hawker centre preparation; formal halal certification should be verified with the vendor. Trans fat at 0.2 g/100g is retained from input and is consistent with commercial deep-frying oil usage documented in HPB database entries for similar fried chicken products.
